analysis:
- Common strong pattern: Pareto prefilter → single‑point rectangle surrogate to shortlist → stamped (lazy) max‑heap that recomputes exact marginal HV only for the heap top → bounded local swap refinement. This balances quality vs HV calls and appears in the top heuristics (2–7).
- Quality boosters: diversity-aware shortlist, larger shortlist multipliers, and pygmo exact HV produce better subsets. Monte‑Carlo fallbacks and sample‑coverage approximations trade accuracy for speed.
- Recurrent inefficiencies in weaker variants:
  - Repeated, expensive hypervolume object construction and redundant recomputation of the same set HVs (no caching).
  - Python-level nested loops for dominance checks and building arrays repeatedly (scales poorly).
  - Overlarge, fixed Monte‑Carlo sample sizes and unbounded evolutionary loops (SPESS) causing unpredictable time.
  - Poor data reuse: frequent np.vstack/array allocation inside selection loops; lost opportunity to reuse masks, sample precomputation, or hashed set→HV caches.
- Good practical compromises seen: cap shortlist size proportional to k, use stamped heap, limit local-swap trials, and precompute per‑point cheap surrogates or dominated‑sample bitmaps to compute marginals cheaply.
experience: Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.
current_reflection: Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.
good_reflections: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates and remove points dominated by the reference. Use lazy greedy with a priority queue storing marginal HV contributions and only recompute when a candidate reaches the top. Cache incremental HV or use per‑point contribution routines instead of full recompute. Combine a cheap surrogate (R2) to shortlist candidates, then refine top ones with exact HV/local swaps. Vectorize mask math and minimize Python loops.

Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter, rect-surrogate shortlist, stamped lazy heap, HV cache, pygmo reuse, vectorized dominance, bounded swaps
- Advice: Prefilter non‑dominated candidates; shortlist ~c·k via cheap rect/R2 surrogate (adapt c to k,N); use a stamped lazy max‑heap and recompute exact marginal HV only at heap top; cache HVs keyed by frozenset(indices) and reuse one pygmo.hypervolume per point set; vectorize mask math and precompute samples; cap local swaps.
- Avoid: Full HV recompute per candidate, rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, unbounded Monte‑Carlo/search effort, and Python loops for dominance checks.
- Explanation: Cuts expensive HV calls, leverages C routines and reuse, keeps runtime proportional to k,N while preserving high hypervolume gain.
